questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: what is Spring Boot auto-configuration trying to do?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: auto-configuration is conditional on the application environment.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Auto-configuration is conditional on the application environment.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Auto-configuration is conditional on the application environment.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, user-defined beans can cause many defaults to back off.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. User-defined beans can cause many defaults to back off.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. User-defined beans can cause many defaults to back off.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Configure common beans based on the classpath, properties, and existing beans.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Configure common beans based on the classpath, properties, and existing beans.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Configure common beans based on the classpath, properties, and existing beans.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: what is a Spring Boot starter?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: starters reduce the need to select every transitive library manually.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Starters reduce the need to select every transitive library manually.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Starters reduce the need to select every transitive library manually.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a curated dependency descriptor for a common capability.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A curated dependency descriptor for a common capability.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A curated dependency descriptor for a common capability.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: A running application server managed outside the project.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A starter is a dependency bundle, not a separate server process.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A starter is a dependency bundle, not a separate server process.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: can a Spring Boot web application run with an embedded server?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Yes, it can package and launch a server with the application.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Yes, it can package and launch a server with the application.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Yes, it can package and launch a server with the application.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: no, every deployment requires a separately installed application server.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Executable Spring Boot applications commonly use an embedded server.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Executable Spring Boot applications commonly use an embedded server.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, an embedded server is suitable only for local development.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Embedded servers are commonly used in production when deployed and operated appropriately.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Embedded servers are commonly used in production when deployed and operated appropriately.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: why is constructor injection often preferred?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, constructor parameters make dependencies straightforward to supply in tests.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Constructor parameters make dependencies straightforward to supply in tests.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Constructor parameters make dependencies straightforward to supply in tests.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: A single constructor can be used without an explicit Autowired annotation.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A single constructor can be used without an explicit Autowired annotation.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A single constructor can be used without an explicit Autowired annotation.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Required dependencies are explicit and can be final.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Required dependencies are explicit and can be final.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Required dependencies are explicit and can be final.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what do Spring profiles control?

answers.text: Short version: Active profiles can be selected through external configuration.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Active profiles can be selected through external configuration.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Active profiles can be selected through external configuration.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Conditional activation of beans and configuration for named environments.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Conditional activation of beans and configuration for named environments.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Conditional activation of beans and configuration for named environments.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: compile-time Java language features.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Profiles are runtime configuration and bean-selection features.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Profiles are runtime configuration and bean-selection features.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what does Spring Boot Actuator add?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Operational endpoints and production-oriented application information.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Operational endpoints and production-oriented application information.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Operational endpoints and production-oriented application information.

answers.text: From what I've seen, automatic public exposure of every endpoint is always safe.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Endpoint exposure and security must be configured deliberately.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Endpoint exposure and security must be configured deliberately.

answers.text: The practical answer is: a healthy Actuator response proves every external dependency is healthy.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A health result covers only the indicators that are configured and included.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A health result covers only the indicators that are configured and included.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: why can self-invocation bypass `@Transactional` in Spring's default proxy mode?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Transactions only work on static methods.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Proxy interception, not static dispatch, is the key issue.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Proxy interception, not static dispatch, is the key issue.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Moving the call across beans or using another transaction mechanism can restore interception.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Moving the call across beans or using another transaction mechanism can restore interception.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Moving the call across beans or using another transaction mechanism can restore interception.

answers.text: From what I've seen, a call through `this` does not pass through the Spring proxy.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A call through `this` does not pass through the Spring proxy.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A call through `this` does not pass through the Spring proxy.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what is the default scope of a Spring bean?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Web-aware contexts also provide request and session scopes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Web-aware contexts also provide request and session scopes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Web-aware contexts also provide request and session scopes.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Singleton within the application context.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Singleton within the application context.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Singleton within the application context.

answers.text: From what I've seen, a new instance for every method call.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Prototype creates new instances on lookup; singleton is the default.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Prototype creates new instances on lookup; singleton is the default.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: can the same Spring Boot property come from multiple sources?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Yes, defined precedence rules decide which value wins.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Yes, defined precedence rules decide which value wins.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Yes, defined precedence rules decide which value wins.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Only `application.properties` can supply configuration.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Environment variables, command-line arguments, and other sources are supported.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Environment variables, command-line arguments, and other sources are supported.

answers.text: From what I've seen, when two property sources define the same key, the lower-priority source always wins.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Spring Boot uses defined property-source precedence, and the higher-priority value wins.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Spring Boot uses defined property-source precedence, and the higher-priority value wins.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: what is needed for Bean Validation annotations to reject invalid request data?

answers.text: My answer: Annotations validate every object automatically when it is constructed.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Constraints are checked when a validator is invoked through the relevant integration.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Constraints are checked when a validator is invoked through the relevant integration.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Binding errors should be translated into a clear client response.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Binding errors should be translated into a clear client response.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Binding errors should be translated into a clear client response.

answers.text: The way I understand it: validation must be triggered, commonly with `@Valid` or `@Validated`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Validation must be triggered, commonly with `@Valid` or `@Validated`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Validation must be triggered, commonly with `@Valid` or `@Validated`.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what is the purpose of a Spring Boot test slice?

answers.text: I'd go with this: `@WebMvcTest` focuses on MVC components rather than the full application.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `@WebMvcTest` focuses on MVC components rather than the full application.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `@WebMvcTest` focuses on MVC components rather than the full application.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: load a focused subset of application configuration for one layer.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Load a focused subset of application configuration for one layer.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Load a focused subset of application configuration for one layer.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, start every external production dependency.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Slices intentionally narrow the context and often use test replacements.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Slices intentionally narrow the context and often use test replacements.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what does graceful shutdown aim to do?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Graceful shutdown makes every interrupted request safe to retry.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Retry safety depends on the operation's idempotency and application design, not shutdown behavior alone.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Retry safety depends on the operation's idempotency and application design, not shutdown behavior alone.

answers.text: The way I understand it: terminate the process immediately without notifying the server.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Immediate termination can drop active work.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Immediate termination can drop active work.

answers.text: Short version: Graceful shutdown waits forever for every request by default.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Graceful shutdown is bounded by configured application and infrastructure timeouts.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Graceful shutdown is bounded by configured application and infrastructure timeouts.
